coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dot-based fluorescence quenching coupled with enzyme-assisted multiple cycle amplification for biosensing of miRNA
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Di
Du, Haotian
Yan, Xiaoshi
Jie, Guifen
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a new fluorescence biosensing platform based on carbon quantum dot (CQDs) quenching coupled with enzyme-assisted multiple cycle amplification was fabricated for miRNA detection. Single-stranded (ss) DNA labeled FAM was used as signal probe, and the small CQDs with good biocompatibility were used as both quenching agent and carrier for FAM, which resulted in fluorescence change. In the presence of target miRNA, the enzyme-assisted polymerization reaction was activated with the help of hairpin primer 1 (HP1), hairpin primer 2 (HP2) and driver primers (DP), forming two double-stranded DNA (dsDNA). Then double-cycle driven-dsDNA nanostructure induced two successive amplification reactions, generating a large amount of ssDNA. When ssDNA hybridized with the FAM-probe and formed dsDNA, it went far away from the CQDs surface and led to the recovery of fluorescence for detection of miRNA. Using this enzyme-assisted multiple amplification strategy, the proposed fluorescence biosensor displays a wide linear range from 10 fM to 10 nM and the detection limit of 4.7 fM. This proposed biosensor shows great potential for early cancer diagnosis in clinic assay.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Multiple cycle amplification
Fluorescence quenching
Carbon quantum dot
FAM-probe

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection mechanism based on CQD quenching and multiple cycle amplification
Feasibility of fluorescence sensing analysis for target miRNA34c
Electrophoresis characterization for enzyme-assisted amplification reaction
Detection of miRNA34c using this method
Study on the specificity of the fluorescent biosensor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22076089 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) X-ray diffraction of cCQD; (B) Fluorescence excitation spectrum of cCQD; (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of FAM-probe (40 nM) under different experimental conditions: (a) Tris-HCl, (b) FAM-probe + cCQD, (c) FAM-probe + cCQD + target (1.0 pM), (d) FAM-probe; (D) Fluorescence emission spectra of FAM-probe upon S1 hybridization in the presence and absence of cCQD: (a) FAM, (b) FAM + target (1.0 pM), (c) FAM + target (1.0 pM) + cCQD.

Polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis of enzyme assistedt multi-recycling amplification system:(a) Mark, (b) 5 μL 1.0 × 10−5 M HP2, (c) 5 μL 1.0 × 10−5 M DP, (d) 5 μL 1.0 × 10−5 M target, (e) DP + HP2 + target (5 μL 1.0 × 10−5 M), (f) DP + HP2 + target + HP1 (no enzyme), (g) DP + HP2 + target + HP1 (with enzyme).

(A)The fluorescence signals responsed to target miRNA34c at different concentrations: (a) 10.0 nM, (b) 1.0 nM, (c) 0.1 nM, (d) 10.0 pM, (e) 1.0 pM, (f) 0.1 pM, (g)10.0 fM, (h) blank. (B) (B) Linearity of the change in fluorescence signal ΔFL versus CmiRNA34c (10.0 fM-10.0 nM) concentration.
FL signal responses for different miRNA sequences (5.0 nmol·L−1). (a) blank, (b) miRNA34c target, (c) miRNA34a (mismatched miRNA), (d) miRNA34b* (mismatched miRNA).
Fluorescence biosensor for nucleic acid detection based on enzyme-assisted multi-recycling amplification and FL quenching by carbon quantum dots.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dot-based fluorescence quenching coupled with enzyme-assisted multiple cycle amplification for biosensing of miRNA

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Optic-electric Sensing and Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, MOE, College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering. Qingdao University of Science and Technology, Qingdao 266042, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Optic-electric Sensing and Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, MOE
College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering
Qingdao University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Optic-electric Sensing and Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, MOE, College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering. Qingdao University of Science and Technology, Qingdao 266042, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a new fluorescence biosensing platform based on carbon quantum dot (CQDs) quenching coupled with enzyme-assisted multiple cycle amplification was fabricated for miRNA detection. Single-stranded (ss) DNA labeled FAM was used as signal probe, and the small CQDs with good biocompatibility were used as both quenching agent and carrier for FAM, which resulted in fluorescence change. In the presence of target miRNA, the enzyme-assisted polymerization reaction was activated with the help of hairpin primer 1 (HP1), hairpin primer 2 (HP2) and driver primers (DP), forming two double-stranded DNA (dsDNA). Then double-cycle driven-dsDNA nanostructure induced two successive amplification reactions, generating a large amount of ssDNA. When ssDNA hybridized with the FAM-probe and formed dsDNA, it went far away from the CQDs surface and led to the recovery of fluorescence for detection of miRNA. Using this enzyme-assisted multiple amplification strategy, the proposed fluorescence biosensor displays a wide linear range from 10 fM to 10 nM and the detection limit of 4.7 fM. This proposed biosensor shows great potential for early cancer diagnosis in clinic assay.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: MicroRNAs (miRNAs) has a vital impact in biological processes such as proliferation, metabolism, tumors and viral infections [1]. Due to the low abundance and easy degradation of miRNA, the development of a sensitive, selective, cost-effective and rapid nucleic acid detection system is urgently needed [2–4]. In recent years, due to the inherent advantages of simple operation, high sensitivity, and good imaging performance for fluorescent sensors [5,6], considerable progress has been made in nucleic acid detection. It is worth noting that the fluorescence sensor is usually based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or a quenching mechanism for detection [7,8]. The system usually contains a fluorophore and a quencher molecule located at two ends of DNA probe, respectively. When the target is absent, the fluorophore molecule tend to approach the quencher molecule with a close distance, leading to the inhibited fluorescence. When the probe DNA hybridized with the target molecule, the quencher molecule and the fluorophore molecule are separated, thus generating released fluorescence signal. Although FRET have been applied in many fields [9–11], it is hard to find the suitable fluorophore and the matched quencher to ensure the satisfied efficiency. In order to solve this problem, various nanomaterials were widely designed and used as quenchers because they can quench some fluorescent groups with different emission wavelengths and improve the signal-to-noise ratio [12]. Although gold nanoparticles [13], transition metal dihalides [14], carbon nano [15], graphene oxide [16] and metal–organic frameworks [17] have already been used as highly efficient nano-quenchers in fluorescence biosensors. However, gold nanoparticles do not readily modify groups, while transition metal dihalides, metal–organic frameworks, etc. are poorly biocompatible [18]. Therefore, it is necessary to find new nanomaterials as substitutes.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a new type of carbon nanomaterial, have attracted increasing attention due to their chemical inertia, high stability, and tunable excitation emission spectra. In particular, CQDs have well fluorescence quenching properties and are able to quench fluorescent small molecules through energy transfer [19]. Moreover, CQDs showed ease of preparation and functionalization [20], small size [21], good biocompatibility [22–23], well photobleaching stability [24], as well as high water solubility, which is very important for the homogeneous sensing methods in nucleic acid detection.

In practical assays, miRNAs used as detection targets have low concentrations of samples. Thus amplification of target miRNAs is essential for sensitive detection, and a multiplexed cyclic amplification strategy can greatly achieve amplification effects on trace targets. DNA amplification strategy has been developed vigorously, especially isothermal nucleic acid amplification technique [25–26]. For example, DNA tweezers, DNA walkers, DNA robots and others are widely used in biosensing [27–29]. With DNA polymerase, DNA primers can continuously grow to achieve stable amplification [30–31], and the specific cutting by endonuclease can effectively reduce the reaction background [32].

In this study, the tumor-related gene miRNA34c as the target has low abundance, so a DNA nanomolecular machine was used to cyclically amplify target concentration, which was further coupled with the biocompatible cCQD to design a fluorescence sensing platform for miRNA detection. MiRNA can induce multi-wheel drive-DNA nanomachine for multiple amplification of trace targets. The cCQD were modified with FAM-probe DNA [19] and quench the signal from fluorophore [33]. The DNA-cCQD interaction is relied on constant competition between electrostatic repulsion and hydrophobic π-π bond interactions. Therefore, ssDNA FAM-probe showed different electrostatic/hydrophobic properties from that of double stranded DNA. When the hydrophobic interaction is greater than the electrostatic repulsion, ssDNA will be adsorbed on the cCQD surface. In contrast, when the electrostatic repulsion is greater than the hydrophobic effect, dsDNA will keep away from the cCQD surface. Moreover, carboxyl groups on CQDs can provide multifunctional anchor points, which has many potential applications in biosensing.

The preparation of carboxyl-carbon quantum dots is based on the published literature with slight modification [27]. 6 g citric acid was put into a dry glass beaker with a lid, and heated at 200 °C in an oven until the white solid particles were completely melt and turned into light yellow. It was kept at this temperature for 15 min, allowing it turn to brownish yellow. Then it was cooled to room temperature. 50 mL of 0.25 M NaOH solution was added to the above solution, stirring it until it is totally dissolved. Following this, the pH of the solution was adjusted to 6.0. After this, a dialysis membrane with a cutting-off molecular weight of 1 Kad was used for further purification. Finally, CQDs solution was treated in a vacuum evaporator at 40 °C to a quarter volume. Then the light-yellow CQDs solution is obtained and stored at 4 °C.

Before use, hairpin 1 DNA (HP1) and hairpin 2 DNA (HP2) were heated in a water bath at 90 °C for 5 min, and then were naturally cooled to room temperature to form a stem-loop DNA structure. 6.0 µL of Tris buffer, 2.0 µL of HP1 (10.0 µM), 2.0 µL of HP2 (10.0 µM), 2.0 µL of DP1 (10.0 µM) and 2.0 µL of target (miRNA34c) (different concentrations) were added to a mixture containing 2.0 µL of 10 × phi29 polymerase Buffer, 2 µL of 10 × NEB Cut smart Buffer, 0.5 µL of phi29 (10,000 U/mL) polymerase, 0.5 µL of N.B.C. (10 U/all) cutting endonuclease, and 1.0 µL of dNTPs (10 mM)) to obtain 20 µL of cyclic amplification reaction system. The above reaction system was reacted in a constant temperature shaker at 37 °C for 3.5 h. Finally, the reaction was terminated at 80 °C for 20 min to obtain single-stranded product (S1).
40 nM FAM-probe was firstly prepared using Tris-HCl buffer. Then the CQDs solution was added to above solution and incubated at 50 °C for 30 min, and CQDs can quench the fluorescence signal.
After adding S1 to the mixture and incubating at 25 °C for 20 min, the fluorescence intensity of the mixture was measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection mechanism based on CQD quenching and multiple cycle amplification
ce:section.ce:para: Enzyme-assisted cyclic amplification strategy was combined with CQD quenching to design a fluorescence biosensor for nucleic acid detection. The design principle is shown in scheme 1 . Two stem-loop structures HP1 and HP2 were designed containing endonuclease sites in loop region. The stem-loop structure exist stably at the experimental reaction temperature, and can hybridize with the corresponding complementary chain. The restriction recognition site in HP1 and HP2 sequences is located in the middle loop region, and the single-stranded swing arm was complementary to the target part and the driving primer (DP), respectively. The driver primer (DP) is used as a fuel molecule, which firstly hybridizes with the swing arm region of HP2 sequence. The stem-loop structure of HP2 is opened, and then further hybridizes with the target. At the same time, the remaining unhybridized fragments in the target sequence tend to hybridize with the swing arm region of HP1, generating the target-initiated two-wheel-driven DNA nanostructure containing HP1, HP2 and DP. Upon the catalysis of phi29 polymerase, the 3′ end of DP, target, and stem-loop structure HP1 continue to polymerize and extend simultaneously, opening the stem-loop structure of HP1. The previous two-wheel-driven DNA nanostructure containing target, HP1, HP2 then split into two double-stranded DNA with nicking endonuclease sites. At this time, nicking endonuclease is added, which can only cut one strand of the double-stranded DNA. The two double strands containing the restriction site are cut at the same time and produce a gap on each DNA strand. In the presence of phi29 polymerase, the gap continues to polymerize and extend from the 3′end to 5′end. This results in the replacement of the single strand of DNA, as well as the re-formation of active site for the the nicking endonuclease on the double strand of DNA. Thus the site will be continuously cut by nicking endonuclease, generating “small gaps”, and the phi29 polymerase continued to bind “small gap” and initiated the strand displacement amplification reaction, realizing the linear amplification of the single-stranded DNA. The above-mentioned cycling reaction occurred only in the presence of the single-stranded target miRNA-34c, generating a large amount of S1. Therefore, the multiple-cycle amplification reaction realized the amplified detection of target.

The fluorescence biosensor was designed based on different adsorption capacity of ssDNA and dsDNA to cCQD. The interaction force between ssDNA and cCQD is stronger than that between dsDNA and cCQD. Initially, the single-stranded FAM-probe binds to cCQD via strong interaction, and the signal from FAM-probe is quenched by cCQD. However, when the FAM-probe hybridized with its complementary strand (S1) to form dsDNA, the interaction between dsDNA and carboxylic cCQD is very weak. So dsDNA tend to move away from the surface of cCQD, resulting recovery of the fluorescence signal, which was combined with the enzyme-assisted multiple cycle amplification of target for sensitive detection of miRNA-34c.

Fig. S1A is the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of CQD, it can be observed that the quantum dots have uniform morphology with an average particle size of about 25 nm. Fig. 1 A is X-ray diffraction (XRD) of carbon quantum dots. It can be seen that CQD has a broad peak at 2θ = 25.7°, which corresponds to plane C (002) [34]. Fig. S1B is the fluorescence emission spectrum of CQD in aqueous solution. The fluorescence emission peak is at about 525 nm with relatively low fluorescence signal. All the results indicate the successful preparation of CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of fluorescence sensing analysis for target miRNA34c
ce:section.ce:para: The adsorption of FAM-probe on cCQD and the quenching effect of cCQD on FAM fluorescence were verified. Fig. 1B and C show that the excitation peak of the cCQD is at 400–550 nm, and the maximum fluorescence emission peak of the FAM probe is around 520 nm, so the two peaks overlap to a large extent. Therefore, there is resonant energy transfer for achieving fluorescence quenching. The fluorescence signal of FAM-probe under different conditions was studied, and the results were shown in Fig. 1C. Curve a showed the low background signal of the reaction system. From curve b and d, we could see that the fluorescence signal from FAM-probe is greatly decreased after addition of cCQD, proving the quenching effect of cCQD on fluorescence signal from FAM-probe.

The feasibility of the fluorescence sensing method based on carbon quantum dot and enzyme-assisted multiple cycle amplification was verified. When the target miRNA34c is further added, in the presence of Nt.BbvCI nicking endonuclease and phi29 polymerase, target-induced signal amplification reaction occurred and produced a large number of single-stranded S1 which is complementary to FAM-probe. The binding interaction between S1 and FAM-probe results in the releasing of FAM-probe from the cCQD surface, and thus generating the recovered fluorescence signal (Fig. 1C, curve c). The experimental results show that the sensing platform for target RNA based on enzyme-assisted target multiple cycle amplification is successfully achieved.

In order to confirm that the complementary S1 does not have any positive or negative effects on the fluorescence signal from FAM-probe, the following experiment is studied. Fig. 1D showed that FAM-probe could hybridize to the single-stranded S1 produced by amplification reaction process. The addition of only target showed no obvious effect on the fluorescence emission of FAM-probe (curves a to b). On the other hand, when cCQD and the target coexist, the fluorescence emission of FAM-probe is quenched (curve c). Therefore, it can be concluded that the single-stranded S1 generated by target trigger shows no effect on the fluorescence signal of FAM-probe, thus the fluorescence quenching effect is definitely from cCQD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrophoresis characterization for enzyme-assisted amplification reaction
ce:section.ce:para: Polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis was used to trace the steps of the enzyme-assisted multiple cycle amplification reaction. In Fig. 2 , lane a is mark, lane b is stem-loop structure HP2, lane c is driver primer (DP), lane d is target. Lane e is a mixture of DP, HP2 and target with equal concentration, and the highest band in lane e stands for the hybridization product of the three kinds of DNA sequence. Herein, HP2 is successfully opened by DP, and the remaining unhybridized part binds with target. Lane f is the mixture of DP, HP2, target and HP1, and the swing arm region of HP1 hybridized with the unhybridized region of the target through base complementary pairing. From the first top band of lane f, we can conclude the success formation of two-wheel-driven DNA nanostructure. In the presence of Nt.BbvCI nicking endonuclease and phi29 polymerase, a large number of produced single-stranded S1 is proved by the bottom band at lane g. Also, from other bands of lane g, we could see the the step-by-step formation of multiple-cycle amplification reaction.

According to the design principle, phi29 polymerase and Nt.BbvCI nicking endonuclease have a very important effect on the fluorescent biosensor. In the reaction system, when both phi29 polymerase and Nt.BbvCI nicking endonuclease are absent, the target cannot initiate strand displacement and chain polymerization reactions, and the cycling reaction cannot proceed. Fig. S2A, B showed the influence of the concentration of phi29 polymerase and Nt.BbvCI endonuclease on the fluorescence signal. In the presence of the target at 10−6 M, and Nt.BbvCI nicking endonuclease at 0.2 U/μL, the fluorescence signal increases continuously with the increasing concentration of phi29 polymerase, and tend to be stable after 0.2 U/μL. Therefore, the optimal concentration of phi29 polymerase was 0.2 U/μL. Subsequently, under 0.2 U/μL of phi29 polymerase, the concentration of Nt.BbvCI nicking endonuclease was further optimized. As shown in Fig. S2B, the fluorescence signal increased with higher concentration of endonuclease. When the concentration of endonuclease reaches 0.2 U/μL or higher, the fluorescence signal of the biosensor tend to be stable. Therefore, the optimal concentration of Nt.BbvCI is 0.2 U/μL.

The effect of reaction time on the fluorescence signal of this fluorescent biosensor was investigated. As shown in Fig. S2C, the fluorescence signal of this fluorescent system gradually increased with the increasing reaction time of the target cycle (10−6 M target). When the reaction time reached 2.5 h, the fluorescence signal of the sensor reached the highest value. Therefore, the optimum time for the cyclic reaction was determined to be 2.5 h.

The optimal amount of cCQD as a nano-quencher for the sensing platform was studied. In a 100 μL reaction system, the fluorescence intensity of FAM-probe with different volumes of cCQD was showed in Fig. S2D. As the volume of cCQD continues to increase, the quenching effect is increased until the volume of cCQD reaches 30 μL. Thus we selected 30 μL of cCQD as the optimal dosage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of miRNA34c using this method
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimized conditions, the biosensing platform was constructed to detect target miRNA34c. As shown in Fig. 3 A, when the target miRNA34c was absent in the system, the cycling reaction cannot occur, and no S1 was produced. Therefore, the quenched low fluorescence signal was showed (curve h). When miRNA34c is introduced to the system, the target initiated the phi29 polymerase reaction, and generated single-stranded S1 hybridized with FAM-probe on cCQD. The formation of dsDNA tended to leave from the cCQD surface, and the fluorescence recovered. This fluorescence signal recovery is related with the increased target concentration (curve g to a). The results show that the biosensing platform could realize the sensitive detection of miRNA.

In Fig. 3B, the fluorescence intensity gradually increased with increasing miRNA34c concentration. The inset showed the standard calibration graph for miRNA34c assay. The fluorescence signal showed a good linear relationship with the logarithm of miRNA34c concentration from 10 fM to 10 nM (y = 18.87 logx + 69.89, where x represents the target concentration, and y is ΔFL, ΔFL = FL-FL0, F, FL0 are the signals in the presence and absence of miRNA34c) with a linear correlation coefficient of 0.993 and a detection limit of 4.7 fM. Five measurements on 10 nM, 0.1 nM, and 1 pM miRNA34c were performed, and the calculated relative standard deviation (RSD) of the detected signals were 2.15 %, 5.4 %, and 2.21 % (Fig. S3). The results indicate that the fluorescent biosensing platform has high sensitivity and good reproducibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the specificity of the fluorescent biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Specificity is a key issue in the detection of this work, because miRNA “family members” have high sequence homology. In order to evaluate the specificity of this fluorescent biosensor, miRNA34 “family members” including miRNA34a and miRNA34b* which is similar to miR34c (target) sequence (Table S1) were used under the same experiments conditions. As shown in Fig. 4 , in comparision to the blank sample, the fluorescence signal of miRNA34c target is the highest, while the fluorescence signals of miR34a and miRNA34b* are very low, indicating that the fluorescence biosensor could distinguish miR34c from other miR34 “families members” with good specificity.

To test the real performance of the FL method in detecting actual sample, a standard addition method was used to measure miRNA34c in human serum sample using the FL method, and 0.1 pM, 1 pM and 10 pM standard solution were added to the human serum sample for recovery testing (Table S2). The obtained recovery by our method was between 96 % and 104 %, showing higher recovery rate and reliability than the reported RT-qPCR assays.
In addition, the comparison of different methods for miRNA detection is shown in Tables S3 and S4, and the results also indicate that our method has good detection performance and higher sensitivity.

A new fluorescence biosensor based on enzyme-assisted multi-recycling amplification and FL quenching by carbon quantum dots was designed for sensitive detection of miRNA. FAM-probe and cCQD were used as signal probe and fluorescence quencher, respectively. The cCQD displayed good biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity, and its functionalization and preparation are easy. cCQD were used as both fluorescence quencher and carrier for FAM-probe, showing multifunctional features. Moreover, the dexterously designed double-cycle driven-dsDNA nanostructure induced two amplification reactions with very high efficiency. Overall, the fluorescent biosensor is simple and rapid, showing good application and promising potential in the field of nucleic acid detection.
Di Wu: Writing – original draft. Haotian Du: Formal analysis. Xiaoshi Yan: Data curation. Guifen Jie: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22076089).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2022.108116.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
